segments.s278-g25: خروشید is the past‑tense, 3rd‑person singular of خروشیدن, narrating that a shout/roar arose. زان پس is an archaic adverbial phrase ('thereupon/after that') formed from زان ('from that') + پس ('after'), setting the temporal sequence. که introduces the content of that cry (a clause). پیروز is an adjective 'victorious' serving as the predicate of the following optative. باد here is the older optative particle meaning 'may it be', so the whole clause 'که پیروز باد …' reports the wish shouted (with its subject supplied by the next segment).
segments.s278-g26: This noun phrase is the understood subject of the optative 'پیروز باد'. سر here means 'chief/foremost' (an epithet), followed by a chain of titles/epithets (خسروان 'kings' used honorifically, شاه 'king' as title, فرخ نژاد 'fortunate‑born/of the fortunate lineage') without overt ezāfe—typical of epic diction. Grammatically the postposed nouns and compounds together designate the person whom the wish concerns: 'the foremost of kings, the king of the fortunate lineage.'
segments.s278-g1: This strings a personal name plus an epithet and a second name: 'فروهل بد' is the name Furuhal followed by the adjective/epithet 'بد' (bad/evil) and then 'و' joins the second name 'زنگله'. In classical Persian the adjective can follow the name as here; the conjunction 'و' simply links the two names.
segments.s278-g2: 'دو جنگی' means 'two warriors/fighters' (جنگی used as a noun/adjective). 'به کردار' is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner of / in the conduct of' and 'شیر یله' is a noun + adjective 'lion (شیر) — yelah/ylah = unrestrained, wild', so the whole phrase says the two fought in the fashion of a wild lion. Word order is noun/adjective and the simile is introduced by the light prepositional phrase 'به کردار'.
segments.s278-g3: The verb 'نبرده' is a negative past form meaning 'had not taken / did not carry'. 'به ایران' marks the destination/target ('to Iran') and the following 'به تیر و کمان' specifies the instrument/objects ('with/by bow and arrow' or literally 'his bow and arrow'). The first 'به' is directional 'to', the second 'به' here functions adverbially/instrumentally.
segments.s278-g4: 'نبد' is an older negative/existential verb meaning 'there was not / did not exist'. It introduces a negated existence: 'there was no one like...'. 'چون' is the comparative 'like/as', 'فروهل' is the exemplar (here a proper name), 'دگر' means 'other/another', and 'بدگمان' is a predicate adjective 'suspicious/distrustful'. The clause thus states the absence of anyone else as distrustful as Furuhal.
segments.s278-g5: The clause begins with the temporal/concessive 'چو' = 'when'. 'از دور' is a locative/adverbial phrase 'from afar' ('از' = from, 'دور' = far). 'ترک دژم را' is the object 'the grim/sullen Turk' (دژم postposes the epithet), and 'را' is the definite object marker. 'بدید' is the past tense 'he saw'.
segments.s278-g6: This is a compound of two actions. 'کمان را' (the bow, object-marked) + 'به زه کرد' — literally 'made/put (it) to the string', i.e. 'strung the bow' (here 'به' = to/toward). 'کرد' is the light verb 'did/made' used with the construction. Then 'و اندر کشید' = 'and drew it inward/back' (اندر + کشید forming 'drew back'). Note that classical dictionaries list 'کشیدن' as 'to pull/draw' even if the provided sense list lacks it.
segments.s278-g7: 'برآورد' here is a past action 'he lifted/brought up' (classical verbal stem برآوردن). 'زان' = 'from that/of that'. 'تیرهای خدنگ' = 'arrows described as خدنگ' (khadang), a compound where خدنگ qualifies تیرها; the phrase means 'dagger-like / piercing arrows'.
segments.s278-g8: 'گرفته' is a past participle 'having taken/seized' modifying 'کمان' ('having seized the bow'). 'رفت' is the simple past 'went' and 'پیشش' 'before him / toward him' indicates direction; 'به جنگ' means 'to battle / into combat'. The sequence describes seizing the bow and advancing to fight.
segments.s278-g9: This clause uses a nominal phrase 'ابر زنگله' ('a cloud / mass of underbrush') as the grammatical subject, followed by the compound object 'تیرباران' and the simple past verb 'گرفت'. Here 'گرفت' functions like 'received' or 'was struck by' (a common classical/poetic use: X تیرباران گرفتن = X be rained/assailed with arrows). Word order: Subject — object (tirobaran) — verb.
segments.s278-g10: The preposition 'ز' (= 'from') introduces the adverbial 'ز هر سو' = 'from every side'; 'کمین سواران' is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the ambushes of the horsemen' (noun + modifier). The final 'گرفت' is again a past verb meaning 'took up' or 'occupied' (they 'took' ambush positions). Word order here is adverbial — object — verb.
segments.s278-g11: 'خدنگی' (a dagger/short spear) is the subject; 'به' marks direction/position ('at/to'), and 'رانش' is used poetically for the side/motion (here 'at his flank/side'); 'برآمد' is a past tense verb meaning 'rose up/appeared'. The simile 'چو باد' ('like the wind') follows the verb and modifies the action.
segments.s278-g12: 'که' introduces a relative clause ('which'); 'بگذشت' is past tense of 'گذشتن' meaning 'passed by' or 'sped past'; the two 'بر' are prepositions meaning 'over/upon' and take 'مرد' (the man) and 'اسب' (the horse) as their complements. 'شاد' is an adjective modifying 'اسب' ('the glad/joyful horse') — the clause describes the dagger's motion relative to man and horse.
segments.s278-g13: The verbal phrase 'به روی اندر آمد' is an idiomatic poetic expression meaning 'came into view / stepped forward (before one's face)'; 'تگاور' is the subject (a warrior). The final prepositional group 'ز درد' ('from pain') explains cause or manner. Word order: locative/idiomatic phrase — verb — subject — causal phrase.
segments.s278-g14: This line combines two short predications. 'جدا شد از او زنگله' is an intransitive clause: 'the thicket/brush separated from him' (جدا شد = 'was separated / fell away'); after the commaless pause, 'روی زرد' is a verbless predicate meaning 'his face (روی) [became] pale' — the copula is omitted, a common poetic device. The two pieces are causally linked to the previous 'ز درد'.
segments.s278-g15: 'نگون شد' is a past passive-like or resultative phrase meaning 'was overthrown / felled'; its patient is 'سر زنگله' ('Zangale's head' or 'the head belonging to Zangale'). The following 'جان بداد' is an idiomatic combination meaning 'he gave up his life' (literally 'gave life' used as 'lost/gave his life'). The two parts are tightly linked: head felled; life departed.
segments.s278-g16: 'تو گفتی' is direct speech framing ('you said'); 'همانا' is an emphatic particle 'indeed/verily'; 'ز مادر نزاد' is a short verbless clause meaning 'not born of a mother' — 'ز' = 'from', 'مادر' = 'mother', 'نزاد' = 'to be born/beget' (negative sense is implied from context earlier in the narrative). The clause reports the addressee's prior statement.
segments.s278-g17: فروهل is a proper name (the subject). فروجست is a past tense narrative verb (classical form meaning 'sprang down' or 'leapt forth') describing the subject's action. و links a second action; ببرید is a classical past form of بریدن (here narrative) meaning 'cut off' or 'severed' and takes سر ('head') as its object (سر follows the verb here as the direct object).
segments.s278-g18: برون کرد is a compound with برون (out/forth) + کردن (کرد), here 'threw out' or 'cast out' in the past. خفتان رومی is a noun phrase 'the sleeping Roman' (خفتان = 'sleeping', used attributively before the ethnonym رومی). ز بر is a short prepositional phrase 'from the saddle/back' (ز = 'from' in poetic diction, بر = 'on/upon' here meaning the saddle/back).
segments.s278-g19: سرش را is the definite object 'his head' (سر + enclitic possessive ش + object marker را). به فتراک زین is a prepositional/locative phrase: به indicates attachment/toward, فتراک 'saddle-cloth' and زین 'saddle' form a double noun phrase meaning 'to the saddle-cloth of the saddle'. برببست is the classical past of بستن with an added be- prefix (here 'he bound/tied'), agreeing with the same subject as the previous clauses.
segments.s278-g20: بیامد is a simple past 'he came' and introduces the next action; گرفت is past 'seized/took' (same subject). اسب او را is the object 'his horse' (noun + possessive + object marker). به دست is an instrumental/locative phrase 'by the hand' (i.e. seized it by the bridle/with his hand); in context it means he grabbed the horse.
segments.s278-g21: به بالا برآمد is a past action phrase meaning 'rose up / sprang upward' (به بالا = upward, برآمد = arose). به سان پلنگ is a simile 'like a leopard' (به سان = 'in the manner of'), placed after the verb to qualify how he rose.
segments.s278-g22: به خون غرقه گشته is a participial clause 'having become drenched in blood' (به خون = 'in blood', غرقه = 'drenched/immersed', گشته = perfect participle 'having become'). بر + تیغ و چنگ links that state to the instruments or means — literally 'upon/with sword and claw' — so the phrase pictures him blood-soaked and armed with sword and claw; the subject is implied as before.
segments.s278-g23: درفش خجسته is the noun phrase 'the auspicious (or blessed) banner/standard.' برآورد is a past narrative verb here meaning 'he raised/lifted up' (classical diction). راست immediately after the verb functions adverbially/adjectivally 'straight/upright' describing how the banner was raised.
segments.s278-g24: شده شادمان یافته is a complex participial cluster: شده 'having become' + شادمان یافته 'having found joy / rejoicing' (یافته is a perfect participle 'found/obtained' modifying the emotional state). هرچ خواست ('whatever he wanted') completes the idea: with that joyful state he 'found/obtained whatever he desired' — خواست is past 'he wished/desired' and here ties to یافته ('acquired what he had wished').

sentences.s278-s13: Then a cry went up: "May the foremost of kings, the king of the fortunate lineage, be victorious."
sentences.s278-s1: Fourth were Furuhal Bad and Zangaleh, two warriors behaving like a wild (unbridled) lion.
sentences.s278-s2: He had not taken his bow and arrows against Iran; there was no other as suspicious as Furuhal.
sentences.s278-s3: When from afar he saw the grim Turk, he strung his bow and drew it back.
sentences.s278-s4: He raised from there those dagger-like arrows, seized his bow, and went forward to fight him.
sentences.s278-s5: A cloud of underbrush was struck by a shower of arrows; from every side the horsemen took up ambushes.
sentences.s278-s6: A dagger rose at his flank like the wind, which passed over the man and over the glad horse.
sentences.s278-s7: The warrior Tegâvar came into view; from the pain the thicket fell away from him and his face grew pale.
sentences.s278-s8: Zangale's head was felled and he gave up his life; you said, truly, he was not born of a mother.
sentences.s278-s9: Furuhal sprang down and cut off the head; he threw the sleeping Roman from the saddle.
sentences.s278-s10: He tied his head to the saddle-cloth of the saddle, then came and seized his horse by the bridle.
sentences.s278-s11: He sprang up high like a leopard, drenched in blood, with sword and claw.
sentences.s278-s12: He raised the auspicious banner straight; rejoicing and glad, he obtained whatever he desired.
